explicit_text: My name is Donald Adolph Adam, born in Waukesha, Wisconsin. I grew up in a family deeply rooted in the brewing industry but found my passion in flying during high school in 1944. I enlisted in the Army Air Corps during World War II and already had my pilot's license when I was called to active duty on my 18th birthday in 1945. After training in Waco, Texas, I became part of a challenging pilot class during the war.

After training in Texas, I became part of a particularly challenging pilot class during the war.

explicit_text: I was born in Waukesha, Wisconsin, a town with an Indian name and a strong German brewing tradition. My family moved there when my grandfather, a brewmaster, was invited to run a brewery in the city after attending a brewmaster's convention in Milwaukee. Waukesha was also the place where I first got my flying experience, including a memorable flight over the town's Five Points area.

explicit_text: My mother was Frieda Hois, daughter of Adolph Hois, a brewmaster. She moved from New Ulm, Minnesota, to Waukesha, Wisconsin, while pregnant with me to join my grandfather at the brewery he managed. She was supportive of my flying ambitions, even signing the permission for me to take flying lessons when my father was strictly against it.

explicit_text: Adolph Hois was my maternal grandfather and a master brewmaster who moved from Wyoming to New Ulm, Minnesota, a town settled by Germans during the Civil War era. He later relocated to Waukesha, Wisconsin, after attending a brewmaster's convention in Milwaukee and ran a brewery there successfully until retirement. He groomed my brother and me to become future brewmasters through the traditional five-year apprenticeship.

Though he was a rugged German who did not have a high school education, he was very strict, especially about my flying lessons, which he initially forbade.

explicit_text: Fox Head was the name of the beer brewed by my grandfather's brewery in Waukesha. It was a well-known and popular beer, and my grandfather even introduced a variant called Fox Head 400, which helped grow the brewery's size and success. Although the brewery eventually closed, it was a big part of my childhood and family life.

explicit_text: During World War II, I volunteered for pilot training while still in high school in 1944 and was called to active duty on my 18th birthday in 1945. There was a significant pilot shortage at the time due to the intense air battles with Germany. My prior flying experience and pilot's license helped me enter the Army Air Corps and undergo training during this critical period.

explicit_text: I joined the Army Air Corps during World War II after volunteering in high school. They required pilots to have at least two years of college, and I was called after meeting that requirement. My training took place primarily in Waco, Texas, flying AT-6 Texan aircraft, and I was part of a notably difficult pilot class with many accidents and dropouts.

explicit_text: Carroll University, formerly Carroll College, was where Army Air Corps recruiters came to find pilot candidates during World War II. They targeted athletes and healthy young men, and they arranged for local flight experiences using Piper Cubs at the nearby county airport. This recruitment effort was part of how I first got exposed to flying and became hooked on it.

explicit_text: Five Points is a notable area in the center of Waukesha where five roads converge. During my first flight in a Piper Cub, I asked the pilot to dip the wing at about 200 feet so I could see Five Points from the air, just like in the movies. That view was unforgettable and helped cement my love for flying.

explicit_text: For my commercial pilot's license, I had to take the test in a Stinson airplane, which was larger and allowed passenger carrying. Since we didn't have a Stinson locally, I went to Milwaukee for the test, including an instrument approach. explicit_text: I rode a Harley-Davidson motorcycle from Milwaukee to Waco, Texas, in two days to get to my Army Air Corps training, even though riding motorcycles was prohibited for pilots in training. During the trip, the voltage regulator failed near the Texas border, but I found a Harley-Davidson repair shop that fixed it quickly. I was very careful to avoid being caught riding during training, as it could have meant dismissal.

It was also the destination of my long motorcycle ride from Milwaukee, which I made despite the rules against riding motorcycles during pilot training.

explicit_text: Randolph Air Force Base was one of the training locations for Army Air Corps pilots during World War II, alongside Waco and a base in Oklahoma for multi-engine training. The class ahead of mine graduated there, and some pilots went on to fly early jets like the F-80 Shooting Star in Phoenix. Unfortunately, two pilots from that class died in a motorcycle accident on the way to Phoenix, which led to a ban on motorcycle riding during pilot training.
